text: The surgeon creates a three-centimeter port in the anterior axillary line at the fifth intercostal space. The incision is extended in the intercostal space beyond the muscle incision. A scope is advanced to the uppermost part of the port, and a lanky rasper is inserted.

The surgeon identifies a lesion in the lung and observes a complete fissure. The lung is retracted cranially using lung grasping forceps held by the secondary assistant, exposing the inferior pulmonary ligament. An energy sealing device and suction are used to assist in the dissection, with the suction providing counter traction.

The surgeon uses both hands to perform dissection, with one hand holding a suction device and the other operating a harmonic scalpel. The assistant retracts the lung using lung parenchymal forceps, allowing for clear visualization and manipulation of adhesions and ligaments.
The surgeon uses a gyroscopic right angle clamp to encircle the inferior pulmonary vein, followed by the harmonic device to remove extra adhesions, creating space for the stapler.

The surgeon prepares the vein and proceeds through the lung fissure to access the artery. Using the harmonic device, the surgeon dissects to expose the common basilar and superior segmental arterial branches, taking advantage of the clear fissure for better visualization.

The surgeon continues the dissection using Damego forceps and suction, maneuvering both hands with advanced instrumentation. The right angle clamp is used to encircle the artery, followed by the application of a curved tip powered vascular stapler to divide the anterior branch. The focus then shifts to the vein.

The surgeon places the suction in the tunnel behind the vein to facilitate the entry of the stapler. After dividing the vein, a small abnormal branch is identified. A 45-degree clip applier is used to place a vascular clip on the branch, followed by the application of the harmonic device to the distal end. The prongus is then lifted up.

The surgeon removes mediastinal tissue and flora surrounding the bronchus before applying a stapler with green reload. The anesthetist inflates the lung to ensure the upper and medial bronchus are not compromised. The freed loop is placed in a retrieval bag for removal from the chest.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy using a systemic nodal dissection approach, starting below the azygous vein to expose the lymph nodes. The dissection proceeds between the lymph nodes and the pulmonary artery, revealing the anatomical structures involved.

The surgeon uses a sucker to elevate the tissue plane and approaches the lymph node station four, aiming to retrieve the lymph nodes in continuity. The dissection proceeds between the azygous vein and the superior vena cava, with the mediastinal pleura being opened to access stations four and two.

The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection near the superior vena cava, targeting lymph node stations two and four in the right paratracheal region. The lung is retracted anteriorly using lung grasping forceps with a peanut, exposing the posterior hilum to access station seven, the subcarinal lymph node. The harmonic device is utilized as a suction tool during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully frees the lymph node at station seven before grasping it, aiming to take it in continuity rather than piecemeal. The bronchial stump and arterial stump are visible, and the surgeon uses a long-armed diathermy and hemostatic agents to manage the lymph node at station eleven.
The surgeon inspects the site of lymph node dissection for any signs of bleeding. A chest tube is placed, and the lung is inflated to ensure proper function before closing the surgical port.
